summary:
Frightful Fey are the class of Unseelie Fey whose bodies, hungers, and supernatural persistence are stabilized by fear. They are not merely frightening creatures, skilled intimidators, or fey that enjoy scaring mortals. A Frightful Fey belongs here when dread is part of its biology: it feeds on fear, grows through fear, breeds from fear, travels through fear, or requires secrecy, nightmare, panic, shame, or helpless expectation to maintain its shape.

Bogey-like and bogeyman-like fey are the anchor examples. This class may also contain nursery haunts, closet fey, nightmare whisperers, red-eyed threshold lurkers, fear-fed child-stealers, hollow-faced watchers, and court-bred terrors used by greater Unseelie powers. It should not absorb every dark fey that has a fear aura. A Dark Hunt Fey may terrify prey, but it belongs to the hunt if quarry-law defines it. A Gloaming Court Fey may use fear as leverage, but it belongs to the court if title and obligation define it. A Blood Fey may frighten victims before the kill, but it belongs to blood if token renewal defines it.

A field crew usually recognizes Frightful Fey through absence and anticipation: a closet door found open after being latched, dust disturbed under a bed, a child’s rhyme repeated in an adult voice, a face glimpsed in a window too high to reach, nail marks inside a toy chest, a shadow that leaves when the lamp is raised, or the certainty that something is watching from the one dark corner no one wants to inspect.
tax:
Frightful Fey sit directly beneath Unseelie because fear-feeding is one of the phylum’s major persistence mechanisms. Where Winter Fey are stabilized by cold and scarcity, Blood Fey by blood-price, and Gloaming Court Fey by witnessed obligation, Frightful Fey are stabilized by dread as an ecological resource. Their bodies are not merely adapted to provoke fear; they metabolize the victim’s expectation of harm.

This class is separated from undead haunts and fiends by origin and persistence. An undead haunt repeats trauma through negative energy or soul residue. A fiend may harvest terror as sin, torment, or lower-planar currency. A Frightful Fey remains fey: glamour-bound, true-name sensitive, cold-iron vulnerable, and tied to living nature’s dark story-logic. Its fear is primal and intimate rather than infernal or necromantic. The thing under the bed is not necessarily dead, damned, or demonic; in this class, it is alive in the manner of fey.

The class is also separated from ordinary ambush predators. A predator hides so prey will not escape. A Frightful Fey hides because being almost known is nourishing. The delay before discovery, the refusal of adults to believe a child, the whispered warning, the ritual of checking a room twice, and the embarrassment of admitting fear are all part of the organism’s habitat.

A descendant belongs here only if fear is necessary to its persistence, reproduction, feeding, or inherited power. “Has Intimidate,” “looks scary,” “uses darkness,” or “causes the shaken condition” is not sufficient by itself.
ap:
Frightful Fey are notoriously difficult to describe because many of them borrow shape from the witness. One person sees a long hand curling around the doorframe. Another sees teeth under the bed. Another sees a sack-faced child with adult hands. Another sees only two eyes in the closet, set too far apart for anything that could fit inside. This variability is a field sign, not a lack of anatomy.

When fully embodied, they tend toward silhouettes that imply incompletion or exaggeration: too tall and folded, too small and old, too many joints, no visible mouth until it smiles, a head wrapped in cloth, fingers like hooked twigs, feet turned backward, elbows where shoulders should be, or a face that cannot be remembered after looking away. Some are almost beautiful until the viewer notices the stillness. Others are crude and nursery-simple, assembled from sackcloth, dust, hair, teeth, buttons, and shadow.

Their eyes are often the most stable marker: red pinpoints, yellow animal shine, black wet beads, moon-pale disks, or absent sockets that nevertheless produce the feeling of being watched. Their voices are secondhand. They speak in borrowed parents, dead siblings, crying infants, remembered bullies, old lullabies, or the victim’s own whisper. Their scent is stale breath, damp wool, closet dust, old straw, extinguished night-lamp, mildew, and the sour-sweet smell of fear sweat.

Spoor includes dust disturbed in places no body could stand, toys arranged to face a bed, fingerprints on the inside of shutters, repeated scratch marks at child height, milk gone sour overnight, animals refusing one room, and sleepers waking with the same phrase in their mouths.
eco:
Frightful Fey persist where fear becomes habitual enough to form a lair. Common habitats include nurseries, closets, attics, cellars, wells, orphan roads, abandoned schools, plague houses, battlefield hospitals, dead-end tunnels, sealed mine galleries, roadside shrines, empty theatres, and bedrooms where no one believed the first witness.

They feed on dread, anticipation, nightmares, secrecy, shame, helplessness, and the repeated act of imagining the creature. A quick scream may be useful, but sustained fear is richer. Many prefer victims who survive long enough to feed them again. A child who checks the closet every night, an elder who refuses to speak of the attic, a guard who hears the same footstep behind him for a week, or a household that pretends nothing is wrong may nourish the fey better than a corpse.

Reproduction may occur by budding from a local fear-pattern. A lesser frightling may arise from a single repeated dread: the thing in the well, the face at the window, the hand under the bed. Stronger forms may mature by collecting names, nightmares, or years of disbelief. Some are born from established fey lines that cultivate fear as deliberately as bees cultivate wax. Others are placed by Unseelie courts as living punishments, nursery spies, or household curses.

Their populations shrink when secrecy breaks. Communal witness, correct naming, daylight inspection, protective household rites, cold iron at thresholds, honest testimony, and adult belief can starve or dislodge them. A Frightful Fey can survive being fought; it is more endangered by becoming known, bounded, and uninteresting.
beh:
Frightful Fey cultivate fear rather than merely causing it. They prefer signs before contact: the door left open, the blanket tugged once, the same rhyme heard from the hall, the missing toy returned with wet hair, the whisper that uses a private name. They teach victims how to fear them, then feed on the lesson.

They are patient but not always intelligent in a human way. Lesser forms may repeat one pattern obsessively until it works. Greater forms understand family dynamics, shame, authority, and the social cost of being believed. Many target isolated victims, especially children, the sick, the grieving, the guilty, and travelers already uncertain of shelter. Others haunt entire groups by making each person think the fear is private.

They dislike confident witnesses. They dislike lamps carried by people who are not pretending bravery. They dislike being described accurately. They dislike children being believed. Their malice often intensifies when a victim tries to rationalize them away, because denial preserves secrecy while increasing fear.

Among other Unseelie, Frightful Fey are spies, punishments, nursery-keepers, blackmail gatherers, and softening agents before bargains. A courtier may send one to make a mortal desperate enough to accept a gift. A Blood Fey may use one to flush prey. A Dark Hunt may use one to mark quarry through nightmares. But a Frightful Fey that feeds too well can become independent of the court that placed it.
traitsText:
Type: Fey.

Senses: Low-light vision. Darkvision is common in descendant taxa. Scent, blindsense, dreamsense, see in darkness, or the ability to locate frightened creatures may appear in lower taxa, but should not be assumed across the entire class.

Languages: Sylvan is standard among intelligent Frightful Fey. Aklo, Common, Undercommon, and local household languages are common where the creature preys on speech-capable communities. Some lesser forms speak only in mimicry, nursery rhymes, repeated victim phrases, knocks, scratching, or breath.

Proficiencies: As fey: simple weapons and any weapons named in the descendant entry. Frightful Fey commonly use claws, teeth, pins, hooked knives, cords, needles, toys, door latches, or no manufactured weapons at all. Armor proficiency applies only when the creature is described as wearing armor.

Defensive Traits: Common descendant defenses include DR/cold iron, concealment in dim light, bonuses on Stealth in domestic or subterranean spaces, bonuses against fear, illusion, or mind-affecting effects, and fast healing or temporary hit points while near frightened victims. These should be assigned at lower ranks.

Weaknesses: Common weakness patterns include cold iron, true-name exposure, public naming, daylight inspection, communal witness, protective household rites, fulfilled guardian-oaths, laughter without fear, and the destruction or cleansing of a fear-site such as a closet, cradle, well, attic, or marked doorway. Some lineages are weakened when their victim stops keeping the fear secret.

Skills: Bluff, Disguise, Intimidate, Knowledge, Perception, Sense Motive, Stealth, Escape Artist, Sleight of Hand, and Use Magic Device are common skill directions. Descendant taxa often receive racial bonuses to Stealth in darkness or cluttered interiors, Disguise when mimicking a feared figure, or Intimidate against isolated targets.

Environment: Any dark or socially fearful site; commonly urban margins, ruins, underground, abandoned houses, nurseries, attics, cellars, wells, mines, forests near settlements, orphan roads, and First World/Feywild borderlands.

Organization: Solitary, pair, nest, nursery-haunt, whisper-clutch, nightmare ring, household infestation, or court-seeded terror.
abilities:
Dread Feeding (Su). Many Frightful Fey gain strength from creatures suffering fear or from victims who knowingly anticipate the fey’s return. Descendant entries may express this as temporary hit points, fast healing, bonuses to attack rolls or save DCs, restoration of spell-like abilities, or growth into a stronger form. The ability should specify what counts as fear, the range or relationship required, whether ordinary shaken/frightened/panicked conditions qualify, and how long the benefit lasts.

Fear Aura (Su). Some Frightful Fey radiate fear in a defined area. In Pathfinder-style terms, fear auras are area effects; if they allow a save, the save is normally Will-based, and fear effects are mind-affecting fear effects unless otherwise stated. Descendant entries should specify range, DC, duration, whether the effect causes shaken, frightened, or panicked, and whether a successful save grants immunity for a period.

Frightful Presence (Ex or Su). Larger or more mythically recognized Frightful Fey may unsettle foes by revealing themselves, attacking, charging, speaking a true threat, or entering a room where their story is known. Pathfinder’s frightful presence convention affects opponents within range who witness the action, usually allows a Will save, often has a 30-foot range, and is a mind-affecting fear effect. Use this only for descendants whose very manifestation is terrifying; not every closet lurker needs full frightful presence.

Closet-Step (Su). Frightful Fey often move through confined, half-forgotten, or fear-associated spaces: closets, attics, wells, cupboards, under-beds, crawlspaces, chimneys, locked trunks, nursery shadows, and the gap behind a door. This may be modeled as dimension door, shadow step, tree stride-like movement through domestic spaces, or a custom supernatural movement ability. It should require secrecy, dimness, fear, or a marked lair; bright witness, open doors, cold iron, or protective rites can block it.

Voice of the Fearful House (Su). A Frightful Fey may mimic voices associated with safety or threat: a parent, child, sibling, victim, abuser, guard, dead friend, or the listener’s own whisper. Lower taxa may model this as sound mimicry, ghost sound, ventriloquism, suggestion, or a racial bonus on Bluff and Disguise. The mimicry should have one flaw a careful listener can notice: wrong breathing, wrong pet name, wrong distance, repeated phrase, or no sound of feet.

Shape of the Witness (Su). Some descendants adapt their visible form to a target’s fear. This is not unrestricted shapeshifting. It expresses details already present in the victim, household, site, or story. Mechanically, this may grant Disguise bonuses, impose penalties on saves against fear, or allow illusion effects such as disguise self, minor image, or nightmare terrain. Communal witnesses, mirrors, daylight, and correct naming should reduce or collapse the individualized form.

Nightmare Intrusion (Sp or Su). Stronger Frightful Fey enter dreams to rehearse pursuit, steal names, mark victims, or feed without physical contact. This may be modeled with nightmare, dream, deep slumber, modify memory, or custom dream-haunting abilities. The descendant entry should define whether the fey needs a known name, a personal object, a previous scare, proximity to the lair, or a sleeping victim already afraid of it.

Secrecy Lair (Su). A Frightful Fey may anchor part of itself in a place where fear is concealed: a closet no one opens, an attic no one discusses, a well children are forbidden to name, a nursery after a disappearance, or a tunnel where survivors lied about what happened. While the lair remains secret or avoided, the fey may gain bonuses, regeneration-like return, concealment, or the ability to reappear after destruction. Public inspection, truthful testimony, daylight rites, or cleansing the site can suppress the lair.

Name-Breaking Exposure (Su). Correctly naming a Frightful Fey before witnesses can weaken it. Descendant entries may express this as suppressing fear abilities, removing concealment, preventing regeneration, forcing the creature into its true shape, or imposing penalties on saves and AC. The name must be meaningful: a true name, repeated story-name, household name, or title by which the lair knows it. Random insults do not qualify.

Isolation Pressure (Su). Many Frightful Fey are stronger against isolated targets. Descendant abilities may increase save DCs, damage, Stealth bonuses, or compulsion effects when a victim is alone, separated from light, asleep, frightened, or disbelieved. The ability should weaken when allies maintain line of sight, speak the victim’s name, share a light source, or physically cross the threshold together.

Guardian-Oath Reversal (Su). Because many Frightful Fey prey on the failure of protection, a properly spoken guardian-oath may repel or damage them. Lower taxa may define this as a weakness, a warding condition, or a way to force a saving throw against the fey’s lair powers. The oath must involve actual protection, not empty bravado; a frightened guardian who stays and names the threat may be more effective than a fearless warrior who treats the victim as bait.
bg:
Frightful Fey matter because they turn fear into habitat. A party can burn a nest, bar a door, or kill a lurking shape and still fail if the fear-story remains intact. The question is not only “what attacked?” but “who was afraid, who knew, who refused to believe it, and what place did everyone avoid naming?”

Village wardens, shrine-keepers, Pall-Brokers, route-makers, and household elders record Frightful Fey signs because the correct countermeasures are social as well as martial. Cold iron may help, but so may shared lamps, public testimony, children being believed, correct names, opened doors, dawn inspections, lullabies with protective words, and refusing to let fear remain private.

This class keeps bogey-like fey distinct from undead hauntings and fiendish terror. Their dread is living fey ecology. They do not simply punish sin, repeat death, or serve evil. They cultivate the primal border between shelter and the dark, between a child’s warning and an adult’s disbelief, between a harmless shadow and the moment it reaches back.
